Some of the content is helpful - it's a very similar approach/philosophy to Allen Carr's easy way. I didn't find that book very compelling and it was the same with the app for the same reasons - minimisation of withdrawal and dismissal of evidence based practice (even medication not just NRT). If it works for you that's great but I personally didn't resonate with it and found another way to quit that is evidence based - as per scientific studies rather than Quitsure's self proclaimed stats.

I am using the paid version of two different quit-smoking apps and I had high hopes for this app. But even though it costs MUCH more than the other (QuitNow! pro) this app is only so-so. It has a nice enough design, but it gives the feel of having just been thrown together so that lots of money could be charged. Here are some issues: 1. Widget isn't stable - keeps blanking out and saying "data unavailabel sign in" and even when the data is there it doesn't update smoothly. 2. Shake the phone when you have a craving, and it's supposed to throw up options to overcome it. This has only worked once on my phone, and only after I shook it so much I felt like an idiot. 3. Configure and track substitutes - well, I have configured the ONE substitute I am using, but there is no way to input my use. So the app is not actually keeping track of my substitute use. (I haven't relapsed so I don't know if it keeps track of cigs smoked, but some reviews have suggested it doesn't do that, either). 4. They say it's based on cognitive behavioural whatsits, but there's no real motivational coaching or anything. The "daily check-in" thing which asks "how are you feeling" seems totally pointless and quite annoying to me: it doesn't really give any good options and doesn't do anything with the info you give (I have turned it off). 5. The only nod to "cognitive behavioural whatsits" is the system of goals and cards. This is okay, I suppose, and the information is useful ("you just got rid of the nicotine in your blood by 50%" and suchlike), but it does feel a bit like a flash-card game in pre-school. 6. The app does not seem to have been designed by native English-speakers, so the use of language is sometimes a bit jarring. Not that this matters much, but I flag it because it just adds to the impression of being hastily put together. Overall, the app is okay, but for a subscription price of $75/yr I would have expected MUCH, MUCH more. Some perosnalised coaching and motivation, guidance on quitting, functions (like the widget or shake for craving) that work seamlessly, proper historical tracking of cig/sub use (with graphs, etc), more intuitive user interface, and so on. So overall, IT'S NOT WORTH THE MONEY.... AT ALL. I would have been willing to pay about $15-$20 for this thing, which is in line with what similar apps are charging.
